titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Potter Stewart Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

Finding aid revision description not supplied.

did.unittitle: Potter Stewart papers
did.abstract: The papers consist of United States Supreme Court case files and supporting documentation, Appellate Court case files and supporting documentation, subject files, correspondence, writings, newspaper clippings, date books, photographs, and audiovisual materials that document Potter Stewart's career as an associate justice of the United States Supreme Court and as a judge on the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals. There is a small amount of documentation and photographs relating to his service on various committees outside the Court and his leisure activities with his family.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been transferred to Yale University for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Potter Stewart Papers (MS 1367). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Potter Stewart was born on January 23, 1915, in Jackson, Michigan, but grew up in Cincinnati, Ohio. He received his undergraduate degree from Yale University in 1937. He was awarded a Henry Fellowship to study at Cambridge University for the 1937-1938 academic year. Stewart then entered Yale Law School, where he became an editor of the Yale Law Journal and graduated in 1941. He worked as a law clerk in the New York firm of Debevoise, Stevenson, Plimpton, and Page from September 1941 to April 1942, resigning to enter the United States Navy. After completing his military service, he rejoined the firm as an associate from October 1945 until July 1946. In 1947, Stewart returned to Cincinnati and joined the firm of Dinsmore, Shohl, Sawyer, and Dinsmore. In addition to his practice, Stewart served two terms on the Cincinnati City Council.

In 1954, President Dwight D. Eisenhower nominated Stewart to fill a seat on the United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit. Three years later, Steward received a recess appointment from Eisenhower to the United States Supreme Court to replace fellow Ohioan Harold Hitz Burton. He was confirmed by the Senate in 1958. Stewart served on the Supreme Court until July 3, 1981, when he assumed senior status and took designated assignments until his death.

During Stewart's tenure on the Supreme Court, he was often asked whether or not he would label himself as a liberal or a conservative. Stewart would reply that he did not think of himself as either one or as a swing justice. At the news conference announcing his retirement in 1981, Stewart elaborated: "I've thought of myself as deciding every case correctly, and I've never thought of myself in terms of putting a label on myself except trying to be a good lawyer and looking at every case under the Constitution and the law, even though I might think it an unwise law. I think it's the first duty of a Justice to remove from his judicial work his own moral, philosophical, political or religious beliefs and not think of himself as being here as some great big philosopher king. It's not a hierarchical organization. Your boss is only the Constitution and the law."

Throughout his service on the Supreme Court, Stewart maintained his pragmatic approach of deciding cases based upon their specific facts. Stewart is perhaps most widely know to the general public for the portion of his concurring opinion in the case of Jacobellis v. Ohio . He stated that, in attempting to define what constituted hard core pornography, "I know it when I see it."

After his retirement from the Supreme Court, Stewart was appointed in July 1983 as a member of President Reagan's Commission on Organized Crime. He died on December 7, 1985, in Hanover, New Hampshire and was survived by his wife, Mary Ann Bertles, and their three children, Harriet S. Virkstis, Potter Stewart, Jr., and David Bertles Stewart.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Potter Stewart Papers consist of United States Supreme Court case files and supporting documentation, Appellate Court case files and supporting documentation, subject files, correspondence, writings, newspaper clippings, photographs, and audiovisual material that document Stewart's career as an associate justice of the Supreme Court and as a judge on the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals. The papers provide a comprehensive overview of Stewart's career from his appointment to the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals in 1954 to his retirement from the Supreme Court in 1981. The material regarding Stewart's time on the Supreme Court from 1958 to 1981 is particularly extensive, as each case that came before the court during that time period is represented in the papers. Substantive correspondence between justices is fairly rare and the correspondence in general is largely routine. The extensive photographs are a good source of information about Stewart's life away from the courtroom.

The Potter Stewart Papers were processed as a collaborative effort between Manuscripts and Archives and the Yale Law School to document the careers and accomplishments of Law School faculty and alumni.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in seven series: I. Supreme Court Papers, 1958-1980. II. Appellate Courts, 1953-1958, 1981-1985. III. Correspondence, 1947-1986. IV. Subject Files, 1951-1986. V. Writings, 1954-1983. VI. Personal Papers, 1918-1986. VII. Audiovisual Materials, 1962-1968.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

Original audio, film, and video recordings, as well as preservation masters and duplicating masters, may not be played. Readers may only play use copies. If a use copy of a particular recording does not exist, researchers must consult with the reference archivist for policies and procedures regarding the creation of duplicating masters and use copies of original recordings.

corpname.part: United States. Circuit Court of Appeals (6th Circuit)

did.unittitle: Supreme Court

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series includes case files and other materials collected or created by Potter Stewart relating to his service as an associate justice of the United States Supreme Court between 1958 and 1981. The case files are divided into two groups: cases in which Potter Stewart authored an opinion and cases in which he did not. The case files are arranged chronologically by October Term, then alphabetically by case name. Although Stewart's office generally used the truncated form of the case name to alphabetize the files, there are some instances in which the case name assigned by Stewart's office is not intuitive. For example, the case "Named Individual Members of the San Antonio Conservation Society v. The Texas Highway Department and the U. S. Department of Transportation" (OT 1970) is filed under "Conservation" as opposed to "Named" or "San Antonio."

The case files document the evolution of a case in the Supreme Court from its acceptance to the publication proofs of the decision. Although the materials in each case file are unique and vary significantly, they generally contain drafts of opinions, correspondence with other justices relating to the drafts, and a printed proof copy of the final decision. They may also include lengthy 'memoranda to the conference' from individual justices outlining their positions on a particular issue, drafts of other justices' opinions, correspondence between Stewart and his law clerks and other justices, and Stewart's handwritten annotations. In some instances, the files contain a series of drafts which document the process of opinion writing in which Stewart circulated a draft opinion to other justices, received criticisms and suggestions, and made alterations to his draft sufficient to create a consensus opinion. Some files do contain early handwritten drafts of an opinion, though notation in Stewart's hand is uncommon. The case files reflect the Court's internal deliberations and provide a glimpse into the individual justice's thoughts about important social topics such as race, school desegregation, obscenity, criminal justice, the Vietnam conflict, and the Watergate scandal.

Supporting documentation, which includes all court materials not included in case files, follows the case files. The documentation is arranged chronologically by October Term, then alphabetically by type. The documentation includes a wide range of materials pertaining to the general workings of the Court and the progress of specific cases. Both the administrative matters that constitute the day-to-day workings of the Court and the weekly conferences in which cases were discussed are doumented. The types of documentation kept by Stewart and the way in which they were filed changed over time. The researcher should particularly be aware that Stewart's office discarded petitions for certiorari and associated materials prior to the 1973 October Term.

The most voluminous type of supporting documentation is court calendars, called docket books. At the beginning of Justice Stewart's tenure on the Court, the docket books were divided into three sets - appellate, miscellaneous, and original. In 1973, the miscellaneous docket book was divided and an additional separate docket book for in forma pauperis cases ('in the form of a poor person,' that is, not liable for the costs of the court) was created.

The docket books consist of a case by case listing, in the order of their assigned docket number, of each matter that came before the Court. Each case was given a page on which was printed the docket number and the full name of the case. Of more interest to the researcher is the voting grid on the bottom of the page. Beside each justice's name, there are a series of Boxes which were used to record each justice's vote on the relevant questions before the Court, including the most crucial issue of whether to allow the matter to be argued before the Court, that is, whether to grant the writ of certiorari. Space is also reserved in the grid for matters relating to jurisdiction, the merits of the case, and other relevant issues. Stewart's notation in the docket books is generally limited to the recording of each justice's vote of whether to grant or deny the writ for certiorari, though he does occasionally make more extensive comments.

Another important set of related materials are the certiorari memoranda which are found in the files denoted 'Appeals, applications, and petitions for writ of certiorari '. These memoranda, by Justice Stewart's law clerks, were prepared to assist the justice with the evaluation of the very large number of appellate cases offered for the Court's review each term. It was the clerk's task to provide a concise introduction to the facts of the case, together with other relevant matters, and then to make a recommendation as to whether the justice should deny or accept the matter for review. Each memorandum concludes with the initials of the clerk who authored the document. These memoranda were generally not circulated to other justices, rather they were for Stewart's use in determining his vote on the threshold question of whether or not to grant the writ of certiorari. If certiorari were granted, then the case would be one of a small percentage of cases accepted for review by the Court each term. Although most memoranda were written by Stewart's clerks, some were prepared by other justices' clerks.
Additional materials relating to Supreme Court cases may be found in correspondence from other justices and law clerks in Series III and newspaper clippings and subject files in Series IV.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains case files and other court material documenting Potter Stewart's work on various United States Circuit Courts of Appeal. The material is arranged in two subseries: Sixth Circuit and Special Assignments. The Sixth Circuit subseries includes case files and other documentation for the period 1954-1958, when Potter Stewart served on the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals. The subseries begins with case files, continues with documentation of Stewart's occasional work on other appellate courts as a visiting judge, and ends with supporting documentation. Supporting documentation is the court administrative material, including calendars, correspondence, and day sheets, and is arranged chronologically. The Special Assignments subseries contains material from Stewart's appellate court assignments after 1981 when he retired from the Supreme Court and assumed senior status. This subseries consists of case files and limited correspondence. The materials in this series are generally routine and do not provide much documentation of Stewart's decision-making process.

Unlike Series I, the case files in this series are not separated by year or into groups of cases for which Stewart wrote or did not write an opinion. Instead, his office sorted them by circuit court, then alphabetically by case name. Included here are materials from Stewart's best known circuit court opinion Garland v. Torres, et al., a 1957 Second Circuit case involving actress Judy Garland in a dispute with the press.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Potter Stewart divided his correspondence into two groups: general correspondence and topical correspondence. The original order has been maintained and the series begins with general correspondence, arranged alphabetically by correspondent, and continues with the topical correspondence, arranged alphabetically by topic. In general, the correspondents in the two subseries do not overlap. Correspondence throughout the series is largely routine. General correspondence includes limited material from Stewart's fellow Supreme Court justices as well as correspondence relating to his service as a member of the Institute for Civil Justice's Board of Overseers and his involvement with the Yale Law School. The materials exchanged between Stewart and his law clerks are the most noteworthy in the topical correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains material related to the Supreme Court and Potter Stewart's activities outside the Court, most notably the various committees and commissions on which he served. It is arranged alphabetically by subject. The majority of the material focuses on Stewart's committee work, highlighted by the material on the President's Commission on Organized Crime and the State Department tours. The series also includes voluminous documentation of Stewart's participation on the Board of Governors for St. Albans School in Washington, D.C.

The Supreme Court material in this series includes clippings from newspapers around the country regarding Stewart's appointment to and retirement from the Court and specific cases heard by the Court. In addition, there are also several files containing documentation of the daily operation of Stewart's office.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series includes articles written, and speeches and a number of interviews given by Potter Stewart. It is arranged alphabetically by title whenever possible, and alphabetically by either publication name or subject when a title is not provided.

Although there is some variance, most Folders contain correspondence with representatives of the host organization or editors, a draft of the articles or remarks, and a final copy. Some files also have multiple drafts of writings, and correspondence with lawyers and others requesting information about speeches made or permission to publish excerpts from them.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series includes photographs, newspaper clippings, date books and awards. It is arranged alphabetically by type. The collection of photographs is extensive in both the expanse of time and activities covered. In addition to formal Court portraits and official event photographs, there are a substantial number of photographs of Stewart and his family. Other photographs are found in scrapbooks and also scattered throughout other Series. The date books are largely routine in nature.

Audiovisual Materials

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of audiovisual material, primarily of interviews given by Potter Stewart. It is arranged alphabetically by title.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy for their personal use should consult Copying Services information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes the following: Lecture 1: Charles S. Desmond (1967 Jan 31), Lecture 2: Paul Freund (1967 Mar 23), Lecture 3. Potter Stewart (1967 Oct 26), and Lecture 4: Potter Stewart (1967 Oct 26).

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: This set of six metal phonograph records, formerly in Box 675 of the Potter Stewart Papers, was transferred to the Historical Sound Recordings Collection, Irving S. Gilmore Music Library, Yale University Library. Researchers interested in access to these recordings should contact Historical Sound Recordings (203 432-1795). There is no longer a Box 675 in the Potter Stewart Papers.
